transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Alex, I’m glad you’re here today. What’s been on your mind lately that feels important to talk about?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, thanks for having me. I guess... I don’t know, it’s just been a bit of a rough patch lately. Things don’t seem to go my way, you know? Like, I was supposed to pick up my girlfriend the other day, and then it started raining out of nowhere. Just felt like one of those moments where everything goes wrong. It’s frustrating. But, I mean, I’m sure everyone has stuff like that happen, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’ve been feeling pretty frustrated with how things have been going lately, especially with that situation with your girlfriend. It can be really tough when it feels like everything is going wrong at once. I hear you mentioning that you think everyone has moments like this, which might suggest you’re trying to make sense of it all. Can you tell me more about how that experience affected you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it was just... annoying, I guess. I mean, I was really looking forward to picking her up, and then the rain just ruined it. I ended up cursing at the weather, which probably wasn’t the best reaction. I felt kind of ashamed afterward, like I shouldn’t have let it get to me so much. I just wanted to be there for her, and instead, I had to come up with some excuse. It’s like, why does this stuff always happen to me? I don’t know, maybe I’m just overthinking it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you were really looking forward to that moment with your girlfriend, and when the rain disrupted your plans, it left you feeling annoyed and even ashamed of how you reacted. It’s understandable to feel that way when things don’t go as hoped. You mentioned feeling like this kind of thing always happens to you, which seems to add to your frustration. I’m curious, what do you think it means for you when things don’t go your way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess it just makes me feel like I’m stuck in this cycle of bad luck or something. Like, no matter how much I try to plan or be there for people, something always gets in the way. It’s kind of disheartening. I mean, I know it’s just weather or whatever, but it feels like a bigger pattern sometimes. I don’t know if it means anything specific, but it definitely makes me question if I’m doing something wrong or if I’m just... unlucky. It’s frustrating, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling trapped in a cycle where things seem to go wrong despite your efforts, which can be really disheartening. It’s understandable to question if you’re doing something wrong or if it’s just bad luck when you experience these setbacks. That feeling of frustration can be heavy, especially when you’re trying to be there for others. I wonder, have there been other times in your life when you felt this way, or is this feeling more recent?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s been around for a while, honestly. There have been moments where I felt like I was doing everything right, but things still didn’t turn out the way I wanted. It’s like I keep trying, but there’s always something that messes it up. I can’t pinpoint exact moments, but I just have this nagging feeling that I’m always on the wrong side of luck. It’s hard to shake off, and it kind of makes me hesitant to put myself out there sometimes. I don’t know if that makes sense.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That makes a lot of sense, Alex. It sounds like this feeling of being on the wrong side of luck has been a recurring theme for you, and it’s understandable that it would make you hesitant to put yourself out there. It can be really discouraging when you feel like you’re doing everything right, yet things still don’t go your way. I appreciate you sharing that with me. When you think about those moments where you felt like you were doing everything right, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s a mix of disappointment and frustration, for sure. There’s also a bit of sadness, like I’m missing out on something or letting people down. I want to be reliable, especially for my girlfriend, and when things don’t work out, it feels like I’m failing at that. It’s tough because I want to be positive and keep trying, but those feelings can really weigh me down. Sometimes I just end up feeling stuck, like I don’t know how to change it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy load of disappointment, frustration, and sadness, especially when it comes to wanting to be reliable for your girlfriend and others. That desire to be there for people is really important to you, and when things don’t go as planned, it feels like a personal failure. It’s understandable to feel stuck in that situation, especially when you’re trying to stay positive. I’m curious, when you think about those feelings of being weighed down or stuck, what do you usually do to cope with them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I guess my go-to is to just kind of retreat a bit. I might grab a beer or something to take the edge off, you know? It helps me calm down, but I know it’s not the healthiest way to deal with things. I also tend to distract myself with other stuff—like watching TV or playing video games—just to avoid thinking about it too much. But deep down, I know I’m not really addressing the problem. It’s just easier to escape for a bit rather than face those feelings head-on.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like retreating and finding distractions, like having a beer or getting lost in TV and video games, serves as a way for you to cope with those heavy feelings. It’s understandable to want to escape when things feel overwhelming, even if you recognize that it might not be the healthiest approach. It’s a common response to seek relief from discomfort. I appreciate your honesty about that. When you think about facing those feelings head-on, what do you think holds you back from doing that?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s a mix of fear and uncertainty. I’m not really sure how to deal with those feelings when they come up, and facing them feels like it might just make things worse. There’s also this worry that if I dig too deep, I might uncover more stuff that I’m not ready to handle. It’s easier to just push it aside and pretend everything’s fine, even if it’s not. I guess I’m just not sure where to start or if it’s worth it to confront those feelings.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a lot of fear and uncertainty around facing those feelings, which is completely understandable. The worry about uncovering more than you’re ready to handle can be really daunting. It makes sense that pushing those feelings aside feels like a safer option, even if it doesn’t truly resolve anything. I appreciate you sharing that with me. If you were to consider taking even small steps toward addressing those feelings, what do you think might help you feel a bit more comfortable or supported in that process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess maybe starting small could help. Like, just talking about it a bit more, even if it’s just with someone I trust. It might feel less overwhelming that way. I think having someone to listen without judgment could make a difference. I’m not sure what else would help, but maybe writing things down could be a way to sort through my thoughts without feeling too exposed. Just something to ease into it, you know? It’s a little scary to think about, but I can see how it might be worth a try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Alex. Starting small by talking to someone you trust and writing things down could provide a safer way to begin exploring those feelings. It’s great that you’re considering ways to ease into it without feeling too exposed. Having a supportive listener can really make a difference, and writing can be a helpful tool for sorting through your thoughts. I appreciate you being open to trying this, even if it feels a bit scary. What do you think might be your first step in this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think my first step might be reaching out to a friend or my girlfriend to talk about how I’ve been feeling. I know they care and would be supportive, so it could be a good way to start. I might also try writing down some of my thoughts and feelings before that conversation, just to get a clearer idea of what I want to say. It feels a bit daunting, but I think it could help me feel less stuck. I guess I just have to take that leap and see where it goes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Alex. Reaching out to a friend or your girlfriend for support and writing down your thoughts beforehand can really help you feel more prepared and clear about what you want to express. It’s completely normal to feel a bit daunted by taking that leap, but it sounds like you’re recognizing the potential for growth and relief in doing so. I’m here to support you as you take these steps. How do you feel about the idea of reaching out and sharing this with them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit nervous, to be honest. It’s always a little scary to open up about how I’m feeling, especially when I’m not used to doing it. But at the same time, I think it could be really helpful to share what’s been going on in my head. I want to be more honest with them, and maybe it’ll help me feel less alone in all of this. I guess I just have to remind myself that they care and want to support me. It’s a step in the right direction, even if it feels uncomfortable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel nervous about opening up, especially if it’s not something you’re used to. Acknowledging that it could be helpful to share your feelings is a positive step. It sounds like you’re recognizing the value of honesty and connection, and that can really help alleviate that sense of loneliness. Reminding yourself that they care and want to support you is a great way to frame this experience. It’s okay to feel uncomfortable; that’s often part of the process. How do you think you might prepare yourself for that conversation when the time comes?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll take some time to write down what I want to say, just to organize my thoughts a bit. It might help me feel more confident when I actually have the conversation. I could also remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that it doesn’t have to be perfect. Maybe I’ll even practice what I want to say a little, just to get comfortable with it. I think having a clear idea of what I want to express will help ease some of that nervousness. Plus, I’ll try to focus on the fact that this is about connecting and being honest, which is ultimately what I want.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful and proactive approach, Alex. Writing down your thoughts and practicing what you want to say can definitely help build your confidence and ease some of that nervousness. It’s great that you’re reminding yourself that vulnerability is okay and that the conversation doesn’t have to be perfect. Focusing on the connection and honesty you’re seeking is a powerful mindset to have. I’m really glad to hear you’re taking these steps. How do you feel about setting a timeline for when you might want to have this conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Setting a timeline sounds like a good idea. I think I’d like to aim for sometime this week, maybe in a couple of days. That way, I can give myself enough time to prepare without dragging it out too long. I don’t want to overthink it too much, you know? Just getting it done will help me feel like I’m making progress. I’ll try to find a time when we can talk without distractions. It feels a bit daunting, but I think it’s a step I need to take.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Alex. Aiming for a conversation in the next couple of days gives you a good balance of time to prepare while still keeping the momentum going. It’s understandable to want to avoid overthinking it, and setting a distraction-free time can help create a supportive environment for your conversation. It’s great to hear you’re viewing this as a necessary step toward progress. How are you feeling about this plan overall?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of nervousness and relief, honestly. It’s a bit scary to think about opening up, but at the same time, I feel like it’s a step in the right direction. Just having a plan makes it feel more manageable. I think once I actually have the conversation, I might feel a weight lifted off my shoulders. It’s like I’m finally taking control of things instead of just letting them pile up. So, while I’m nervous, I’m also hopeful that it’ll lead to something positive.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel that mix of nervousness and relief. Taking that step to open up can feel daunting, but it’s encouraging to hear that you see it as a move toward taking control of your feelings and experiences. That sense of hope for a positive outcome is really powerful. It sounds like you’re ready to embrace this opportunity for connection and growth. As you prepare for this conversation, remember to be gentle with yourself. If you ever want to talk more about it or anything else, I’m here to support you. How do you feel about checking in again after your conversation to reflect on how it went?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in after the conversation would be really helpful. It would give me a chance to reflect on how it went and maybe talk about how I’m feeling afterward. I appreciate that support. It feels good to know I have someone to talk to about it. I’ll definitely keep that in mind. Just having this plan in place makes me feel a bit more confident about the whole thing. Thanks for being here for me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that, Alex. It’s great that you see the value in checking in afterward to reflect on your experience and feelings. Having that support can make a big difference. I appreciate your openness and willingness to explore these feelings together. It sounds like you’re taking meaningful steps forward, and I’m here for you whenever you need to talk. Just remember to be kind to yourself throughout this process. Is there anything else on your mind today that you’d like to share before we wrap up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve shared quite a bit for today. I really appreciate the space to talk about all of this. It’s nice to feel like I’m not just carrying it all by myself. I’ll definitely keep everything we talked about in mind as I prepare for the conversation. Just having this discussion has helped me feel a bit lighter. Thanks for being so understanding. I’ll look forward to checking in after I talk with my girlfriend.
